sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board is split vertically by a white column divider at col 32-33. Left side has a dark gray background with several colored objects. Right side has a black background with objects. Need to understand the object layout and goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so the light green square is now at bottom-right [42:43, 12:13]. The level hasn't completed.
Let me think about this differently. I've been trying to match the "stepped" pattern, but maybe the goal is something else.
Let me re-examine the game. The right panel has two shapes:
- Red shape: "stepped" pattern
- Blue shape: "stepped" pattern
The left panel has:
- Colored blocks (gray, red, dark red, blue, checkerboard)
- Two gray boxes with colored squares (yellow and light green)
Maybe the game is about "copying" the right shapes to the left side. The colored squares are "stamps" that copy the pattern from the right shapes to the left side.
But I haven't seen any evidence of the blocks being painted. The blocks just rearrange when I click on the right shapes.
Let me think about the game differently. Maybe the goal is to arrange the left blocks to match the right shapes, and the colored squares are just indicators of which "layer" or "row" is currently active.
Looking at the right shapes:
- Red shape: 6 rows, 15 cols. Top 3 rows are 7 wide (centered), bottom 2 rows are 13 wide.
- Blue shape: 6 rows, 15 cols. Same pattern.
The left blocks need to form a similar pattern. The blocks are:
- Gray: 4x4
- Red: 4x6 (horizontal) or 6x4 (vertical)
- Dark red: 4x4
- Blue: 4x4
- Checkerboard: 4x4
The current configuration has:
- Checkerboard: [20:23, 18:21] (4x4) - top-right
- Gray: [30:33, 8:11] (4x4) - middle-left
- Red: [30:33, 12:17] (4x6) - middle-center
- Dark red: [30:33, 18:21] (4x4) - middle-right
- Blue rect: [34:37, 8:11] (4x4) - bottom-left
This forms:
- Top: checkerboard (4x4) at right
- Middle: gray + red + dark red (4x14)
- Bottom: blue rect (4x4) at left
Hmm, this doesn't match the "stepped" pattern. Let me think about what configuration would match the pattern.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the right shapes more carefully. The shapes are:
- Red shape: rows 16-21, cols 40-54
  - Row 16: BBBBWWWWWWWWWBBBB (white frame)
  - Row 17: BBBBWRRRRRRRWBBBB (7 red)
  - Row 18: BBBBWRRRRRRRWBBBB (7 red)
  - Row 19: BWWWWRRRRRRRWWWWB (9 red)
  - Row 20: BWRRRRRRRRRRRRRWB (13 red)
  - Row 21: BWRRRRRRRRRRRRRWB (13 red)
  - Row 22: BWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWB (white frame)
Wait, the initial state had white frames around the shapes. After clicking on the shapes, the white frames disappeared. So the current shapes are:
- Red shape: rows 17-21, cols 42-54 (approximately)
  - Row 17: 7 red (centered)
  - Row 18: 7 red (centered)
  - Row 19: 9 red (centered)
  - Row 20: 13 red (centered)
  - Row 21: 13 red (centered)
This is a "stepped" pattern that widens as it goes down.
The left blocks need to form a similar pattern. The blocks are:
- Gray: 4x4
- Red: 4x6 (horizontal) or 6x4 (vertical)
- Dark red: 4x4
- Blue: 4x4
- Checkerboard: 4x4
The total area is 16 + 24 + 16 + 16 + 16 = 88 pixels. The red shape has 7*2 + 9 + 13*2 = 14 + 9 + 26 = 49 pixels. The blue shape has the same area. So the left blocks don't match the right shapes' area.
Hmm, maybe the goal is not to match the right shapes exactly. Maybe the goal is to arrange the left blocks in a specific configuration, and the right shapes are just visual hints.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the game is about matching the colors of the left blocks to the right shapes. The red shape is red, and the blue shape is blue. The left blocks include a red block and a blue block.
Maybe the goal is to position the red block to match the red shape, and the blue block to match the blue shape. The red shape is at the top-right of the right panel, and the blue shape is at the bottom-right. The red block needs to be at the top-right of the left panel, and the blue block needs to be at the bottom-left.
But the current configuration has the red block in the middle and the blue block at the bottom-left. The red block is not at the top-right.
Let me think about this more carefully. The right shapes are:
- Red shape: top-right of right panel
- Blue shape: bottom-right of right panel
The left blocks are:
- Red block: middle of left panel
- Blue block: bottom-left of left panel
Maybe the goal is to position the red block at the top-right and the blue block at the bottom-left, matching the right shapes' positions.
But the red block can only be horizontal (4x6) or vertical (6x4). It can't be moved to the top-right position.
Wait, maybe I'm overthinking this. Let me try a different approach. Maybe the game is about clicking on the right shapes in a specific order, and the level completes after clicking on all the shapes in the correct order.
Let me try clicking on the red shape and then the blue shape, and see if the level completes.
Actually, I've already clicked on both shapes multiple times. The level hasn't completed.
Let me try yet another approach. Maybe the game is about positioning the colored squares to match specific positions in the gray boxes. The right shapes have a "stepped" pattern, and the colored squares need to be positioned to match this pattern.
The "stepped" pattern has:
```
  . X .
  . X .
  X X X
```
The yellow square is at middle-center, which is one of the "X" cells. The light green square is at bottom-right, which is also one of the "X" cells.
But the level hasn't completed. Maybe I need to move the yellow square to a different position as well.
Let me try moving the yellow square using the arrow keys. But wait, the arrow keys only move the light green square, not the yellow square. The yellow square is in a different gray box.
Hmm, maybe I need to click on the yellow square to select it, and then use the arrow keys to move it. Or maybe I need to click on the gray box to select the square inside it.
Let me try clicking on the top gray box (where the yellow square is) and see if it selects the yellow square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the top gray box to select the yellow square
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click on top gray box to select yellow square')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 24}])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Check yellow square position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Yellow: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

# Check light green position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Light green: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try moving UP to see if yellow square moves
predict(expect_change=True, note='Try UP to move yellow square')
action(['UP'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Yellow after UP: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
